4  Loading and Exploring the Data

4.1 Learning Objectives

After completing this chapter, you should be able to

  • load one WISDM phone accelerometer file,
  • load all participant files into one DataFrame,
  • map activity codes to readable activity names,
  • restrict the dataset to selected target activities,
  • inspect class distribution and subject distribution,
  • compute and visualize acceleration magnitude,
  • estimate the sampling rate from timestamps.

4.2 Why This Chapter Matters

Before we create windows, extract features, or train a model, we need to make sure that we can load and understand the raw data.

This chapter turns a folder of raw text files into a structured table that we can analyze and process further.

TipEngineering Insight

A clean data loading step is one of the most important parts of a reproducible machine learning project.

If the loading logic is unclear or inconsistent, every later result becomes questionable.

4.3 Step 1 — Import Required Libraries

4.3.1 Goal

We import the libraries needed for data loading, numerical processing and visualization.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

4.3.2 Expected Output

This cell should run without errors.

4.4 Step 2 — Configure Project Paths

4.4.1 Goal

We define where the project root and the data directory are located.

current_path = Path.cwd().resolve()

for parent in [current_path] + list(current_path.parents):
    if (parent / "app").exists():
        PROJECT_ROOT = parent
        break
else:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not find project root containing app/ directory.")

sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT))

from app.config import DATA_DIR
from app.data_loader import (
    ACTIVITY_MAP,
    TARGET_ACTIVITIES,
    load_single_wisdm_file,
    load_wisdm_phone_accel,
)
from app.pipeline import add_magnitude


print("Project root:", PROJECT_ROOT)
print("Data directory:", DATA_DIR)
Project root: /Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook
Data directory: /Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data

4.4.2 Expected Output

The output should point to your project folder and the data/ directory.

WarningCommon Pitfall

If the printed path does not point to your project folder, you may have started Quarto from the wrong directory.

4.5 Step 3 — Define Activity Mapping

4.5.1 Goal

The raw WISDM files use activity codes such as A, D, and E.

We map these codes to readable names.

ACTIVITY_MAP
{'A': 'walking',
 'B': 'jogging',
 'C': 'stairs',
 'D': 'sitting',
 'E': 'standing',
 'F': 'typing',
 'G': 'brushing_teeth',
 'H': 'eating_soup',
 'I': 'eating_chips',
 'J': 'eating_pasta',
 'K': 'drinking',
 'L': 'eating_sandwich',
 'M': 'kicking',
 'O': 'catch',
 'P': 'dribbling',
 'Q': 'writing',
 'R': 'clapping',
 'S': 'folding_clothes'}
TARGET_ACTIVITIES
['walking', 'sitting', 'standing']

4.5.2 Discussion

We keep the complete mapping even though we currently use only three activities.

This makes it easier to extend the project later.

4.6 Step 4 — Inspect Available Files

4.6.1 Goal

Before loading the data, we check which raw files are available.

files = sorted(DATA_DIR.glob("data_*_accel_phone.txt"))

len(files), files[:5]
(51,
 [PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1600_accel_phone.txt'),
  PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1601_accel_phone.txt'),
  PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1602_accel_phone.txt'),
  PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1603_accel_phone.txt'),
  PosixPath('/Users/konak/HPI/Vorlesungen/Data Engineering for Wearables SS2026/Mini-Project/de4w-engineering-workbook/data/data_1604_accel_phone.txt')])

4.6.2 Expected Output

You should see approximately 51 files, one per participant.

4.7 Step 5 — Load One Participant File

4.7.1 Goal

We first load a single file to understand the raw structure.

load_single_wisdm_file
<function app.data_loader.load_single_wisdm_file(file_path: pathlib.Path) -> pandas.DataFrame>
df_one = load_single_wisdm_file(files[0])

df_one.head()
subject_id activity_code timestamp x y z activity
0 1600 A 252207666810782 -0.364761 8.793503 1.055084 walking
1 1600 A 252207717164786 -0.879730 9.768784 1.016998 walking
2 1600 A 252207767518790 2.001495 11.109070 2.619156 walking
3 1600 A 252207817872794 0.450623 12.651642 0.184555 walking
4 1600 A 252207868226798 -2.164352 13.928436 -4.422485 walking

4.7.2 Expected Output

You should see a table with the columns:

subject_id, activity_code, timestamp, x, y, z, activity

4.8 Step 6 — Load All Participant Files

4.8.1 Goal

We now load all phone accelerometer files and combine them into a single DataFrame.

load_wisdm_phone_accel
<function app.data_loader.load_wisdm_phone_accel(data_dir: pathlib.Path, target_activities: collections.abc.Sequence[str] | None = ['walking', 'sitting', 'standing']) -> pandas.DataFrame>
df = load_wisdm_phone_accel(
    DATA_DIR,
    target_activities=TARGET_ACTIVITIES,
)

df.head()
subject_id activity_code timestamp x y z activity
0 1600 A 252207666810782 -0.364761 8.793503 1.055084 walking
1 1600 A 252207717164786 -0.879730 9.768784 1.016998 walking
2 1600 A 252207767518790 2.001495 11.109070 2.619156 walking
3 1600 A 252207817872794 0.450623 12.651642 0.184555 walking
4 1600 A 252207868226798 -2.164352 13.928436 -4.422485 walking

4.9 Step 7 — Inspect the Dataset

4.9.1 Goal

We check the size, classes and number of subjects.

df.shape
(814013, 7)
df["activity"].value_counts()
activity
walking     279817
standing    269604
sitting     264592
Name: count, dtype: int64
df["subject_id"].nunique()
51

4.9.2 Discussion

At this point, we should verify that

  • all expected subjects were loaded,
  • all target activities are present,
  • the class distribution is not extremely imbalanced.

4.10 Step 8 — Add Acceleration Magnitude

4.10.1 Goal

We compute the magnitude of the three-dimensional acceleration vector.

\[ m = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} \]

df = add_magnitude(df)

df.head()
subject_id activity_code timestamp x y z activity magnitude
0 1600 A 252207666810782 -0.364761 8.793503 1.055084 walking 8.864082
1 1600 A 252207717164786 -0.879730 9.768784 1.016998 walking 9.860900
2 1600 A 252207767518790 2.001495 11.109070 2.619156 walking 11.587812
3 1600 A 252207817872794 0.450623 12.651642 0.184555 walking 12.661010
4 1600 A 252207868226798 -2.164352 13.928436 -4.422485 walking 14.773088

4.10.2 Discussion

The magnitude combines all three axes into one signal.

It is often easier to interpret than individual axes because it is less sensitive to phone orientation.

4.11 Step 9 — Plot One Activity

4.11.1 Goal

We visualize x, y, z and magnitude for one activity.

activity = "walking"

sample = (
    df[df["activity"] == activity]
    .head(400)
    .reset_index(drop=True)
)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 4))
plt.plot(sample["x"], label="x")
plt.plot(sample["y"], label="y")
plt.plot(sample["z"], label="z")
plt.plot(sample["magnitude"], label="magnitude", linewidth=2)

plt.title(f"Phone accelerometer signal: {activity}")
plt.xlabel("Sample")
plt.ylabel("Acceleration [m/s²]")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

4.11.2 Discussion

Walking usually produces a more dynamic and periodic signal than sitting or standing.

4.12 Step 10 — Compare Activities

4.12.1 Goal

We compare the magnitude signal for walking, sitting and standing.

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))

for activity in TARGET_ACTIVITIES:
    signal = (
        df[df["activity"] == activity]["magnitude"]
        .head(400)
        .reset_index(drop=True)
    )
    plt.plot(signal, label=activity)

plt.title("Magnitude comparison")
plt.xlabel("Sample")
plt.ylabel("Magnitude")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

4.12.2 Discussion

Walking should usually be easier to distinguish because it contains stronger and more regular movement.

Sitting and standing are more similar and may be harder to separate.

4.13 Step 11 — Estimate the Sampling Rate

4.13.1 Goal

The WISDM documentation states that the data was collected at approximately 20 Hz.

We estimate the sampling rate from timestamps.

subject = df["subject_id"].iloc[0]
activity = "walking"

sample = df[
    (df["subject_id"] == subject)
    & (df["activity"] == activity)
].sort_values("timestamp")

dt = sample["timestamp"].diff().dropna()

sampling_rate = 1e9 / dt.mean()

sampling_rate
np.float64(19.85939378331699)

4.13.2 Expected Output

The result should be close to 20 Hz.

NoteRemember

Documentation is important, but data engineers should still verify assumptions directly from the data.

4.14 Checkpoint

Before continuing, you should be able to answer:

  1. Why do we load one file before loading all files?
  2. Why do we keep the subject identifier?
  3. What is acceleration magnitude?
  4. Why are sitting and standing harder to distinguish than walking?
  5. Why is the sampling rate important for the next chapter?

4.15 Further Exploration

Optional: modify the target activity selection so that the dataset also includes jogging.

Which parts of the pipeline would change?

4.16 Summary

In this chapter you learned how to

  • load one raw WISDM file,
  • load all participant files,
  • map activity codes,
  • inspect class and subject distributions,
  • compute acceleration magnitude,
  • visualize raw signals,
  • estimate sampling rate.

In the next chapter we will transform the continuous sensor stream into fixed-size windows.

3  Understanding the Dataset
5  Windowing
